Other Formats

  • PDF

Multi-Omic Analysis

Author

Antoine Chaillon

Published

April 11, 2026

1. Multi-Omic Normalization of the HIV Signature

Data Harmonization & Subsetting Logic

To isolate the long-term impact of cannabis on the inflammatory “milieu,” transcriptomic analysis was restricted to Vehicle-treated MDM samples (\(n=36\)). This ensures that the observed signatures reflect the donor’s historical in vivo exposure rather than acute ex vivo stimulation. Proteomic data was derived from matching donor plasma (\(n=26\)).

For multi-omic validation, MDM transcripts were mapped to plasma proteins using a one-to-many Ensembl-to-Symbol relational map, with multiple transcripts for a single symbol collapsed to the mean:

\[\bar{x}_{\text{Symbol}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{ENSEMBL}_{i}\]

1.1 Establishing the Basal HIV Signature

To understand how cannabis modulates the macrophage environment, we first established the “Basal HIV Signature.” This was defined by comparing HIV+ and HIV- donors who were both cannabis-naive, using only the Vehicle-treated samples (\(n=10\)).

The signature is characterized by significant upregulation in TNF\(\alpha\) Signaling, Macrophage Activation, and Neuroinflammation (table 1 below).

Note

Here I am using the pathways identified in the latest R code from Claude AI you shared on APR-10.

1.2 Defined Pathways of Interest

1.2 The Transcriptomic “Sign-Flip”: Normalizing the HIV Environment

Cannabis-Induced Normalization

We then evaluated the effect of historical cannabis exposure (Moderate, Daily, and Combined “Any User”) on these specific basal pathways. We observed a nearly universal “sign-flip,” where pathways natively upregulated by HIV were significantly suppressed in cannabis users

This table highlights the primary pathways upregulated in the HIV+ basal state (Blue columns) alongside their corresponding enrichment in cannabis users (Red columns). Data represents Normalized Enrichment Scores (NES) with associated p-values. A negative NES in the cannabis column represents a shift toward the transcriptional profile of an HIV-negative donor.

Global Transcriptomic Normalization Map

A comparison of the HIV Disease Signature (x-axis; HIV+ Naive vs HIV- Naive) against the Cannabis Treatment Effect (y-axis; HIV+ User vs HIV+ Naive). Pathways situated in the bottom-right quadrant represent biological processes induced by HIV infection that are subsequently suppressed by cannabis exposure. The dotted red line (\(y = -x\)) represents an ideal “perfect reversal.” The size of each point corresponds to the statistical significance of the cannabis-mediated suppression (\(-log10(p)\)).

1.3 Multi-Omic Validation: From Source to System

To verify that the transcriptomic suppression observed in our ex vivo MDM model reflects the systemic inflammatory state of the patients, we correlated the MDM RNA expression with the donors’ circulating plasma protein levels.

Statistical Approach

The “Source-to-System” concordance was calculated using Pearson’s correlation coefficient (\(r\)) for lead-edge genes identified in the GSEA. For genes with multiple transcripts, we utilized the mean expression value (see equation above).

1.4 Biological Proof-of-Concept: The TRAF1/TNF Axis

As a representative example of this normalization, TRAF1 (a key mediator of the TNF\(\alpha\) signaling pathway) showed both high transcriptomic reversal (\(NES = -2.4\)) and a strong systemic correlation (\(r = 0.62\)).

In the plasma, we observe a significant reduction in TRAF1 protein levels in both Moderate and Daily cannabis users compared to Naive HIV+ donors.

1.5 Summary of Multi-Omic Concordance

The following table summarizes the markers that successfully bridge the cellular and systemic datasets.

Correlation (r) indicates the strength of the relationship between MDM cellular activity and patient blood levels. Cannabis Reversal (NES) indicates the magnitude of pathway suppression in HIV+ cannabis users; negative values (Blue) represent a shift toward a “normal” (HIV-negative) transcriptomic state.

2. Altered Cellular Responsiveness

The goal here is to determine if a macrophage from a cannabis user is “primed” differently, making it more or less responsive to inflammatory stimuli (IL1B) or cannabinoids (THC/CBD) added in the lab.

2.1 Conserved Transcriptional Program Following Acute IL-1β Challenge

To determine if chronic cannabis exposure results in “myeloid exhaustion” or altered sensitivity, we analyzed the acute response to inflammatory (IL-1β) and cannabinoid (THC, CBD) stimuli using a Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT).

We first analyzed the “Activation Potential” of MDMs by stimulating them with IL-1β. We modeled the response within each donor group to see if the magnitude of inflammatory induction differs.The response is defined by the log2 fold change (\(\text{LFC}\)) of gene expression (\(G\)) under stimulation (\(\text{stim}\)) compared to the vehicle (\(\text{veh}\)):

\[\text{LFC}\_{\text{IL1B}} = \log_2(G\_{\text{stim}}) - \log_2(G\_{\text{veh}})\]

Key Finding: Functional Competence is Maintained. While chronic cannabis exposure normalizes the basal inflammatory state (Section 3), it is critical to determine if this results in “immune paralysis.” We utilized a Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT) to identify genes with divergent responses to acute IL-1β stimulation.

The absence of genes reaching global significance (\(FDR < 0.05\)) indicates that the “Transcriptomic Normalization” associated with cannabis use does not result in myeloid exhaustion or a blunted capacity to respond to acute stimuli.

Figure 3 demonstrates a high degree of concordance in the activation programs across all donor groups. The tight alignment along the \(y=x\) diagonal proves that the primary activation pathways remain functionally intact, regardless of historical cannabis exposure.

2.2 Divergence in Regulatory and Adhesion Markers

While the core inflammatory program remained stable, a subset of genes showed nominal evidence of differential responsiveness (\(p < 0.01\)). Notably, ITGA1 (Integrin Alpha-1) and LILRB1 (Leukocyte Immunoglobulin Like Receptor B1) exhibited blunted induction in cannabis users.

The \(log_2\) Fold Change represents the “Interaction Effect.” A negative value (e.g., ITGA1: \(-4.73\)) indicates that the gene’s response to IL-1β was significantly attenuated in cannabis users compared to naive donors.

2.3 Visualizing Global Response Conservation

2.4 Acute Sensitivity to Cannabinoids (THC & CBD)

Now, we apply the same logic to THC and CBD. We want to know: Do the macrophages of daily users “care” less when you give them THC in the dish?

The “Tolerance” Hypothesis: If chronic users are desensitized, we expect to see a “blunted” diagonal in the concordance plot (a slope \(< 1\)) or a large cluster of genes with significant interaction terms.

The “Nominal Hits” represent genes where the transcriptional response to acute challenge differed between cannabis users and naive donors at a non-adjusted threshold of \(p < 0.01\). The extremely low frequency of such events (4 genes out of ~15,000 tested) indicates that chronic cannabis exposure does not induce significant cellular tolerance or functional exhaustion in macrophages.

Temptative discussion (AI)

In summary, these data define cannabis as a selective homeostatic modulator in the context of HIV: it successfully ‘reverses’ the chronic disease-associated transcriptome (Section 1) without compromising the innate immune system’s capacity to mount an acute response to secondary inflammatory or pharmacological stimuli (Section 2)

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| show: false

library(here)
source(here("functions/lib.R"))
source(here("functions/fun.R"))
library(tibble)
library(gt)
library(DESeq2)
library(tidyverse)


theme_gt_size_color<- function(gt_table, color = "#5B82D8",ftsize="small") {
        gt_table %>%
                # Set smaller font size and compact row spacing
                gt::tab_options(
                        table.font.size = ftsize,
                        data_row.padding = gt::px(3),
                        heading.padding = gt::px(4),
                        summary_row.padding = gt::px(3),
                        row.striping.include_table_body = TRUE,
                        table_body.hlines.color = alpha(color,0.8),
                        row.striping.background_color = alpha(color,0.2)  # apply custom color here
                ) %>%
                # Apply compact styling to column labels
                gt::tab_style(
                        style = list(
                                gt::cell_text(size = "small", weight = "bold"),
                                gt::cell_borders(sides = "bottom", color = color, weight = gt::px(1))
                        ),
                        locations = gt::cells_column_labels()
                )
}


sampleTable<- readRDS(here("results","sampleTable.all.RDS"))
# txi <- readRDS(here("results","txi_all.RDS"))
# coldata <- readRDS(here("results","coldata.RDS"))
gene_map <- readRDS(here("results","genemap.RDS"))

# res_table_plot <- readRDS(here("results","res_table_plot.RDS"))
# dds<- readRDS(here("results","dds.RDS"))



# vsd<- readRDS(here("results","vsd.RDS"))

GO_plot_list <- readRDS(here("results","GO_plot_list.RDS"))
rlog_out <- readRDS(here("results","rlog_out.RDS"))


outpath=file.path(here("outputs",Sys.Date(),"/"))
dir.create(outpath)

```

# 1. Multi-Omic Normalization of the HIV Signature

**Data Harmonization & Subsetting Logic**

To isolate the long-term impact of cannabis on the inflammatory "milieu," transcriptomic analysis was restricted to **Vehicle-treated MDM samples** ($n=36$). This ensures that the observed signatures reflect the donor's historical in vivo exposure rather than acute ex vivo stimulation. Proteomic data was derived from matching donor plasma ($n=26$).

For multi-omic validation, MDM transcripts were mapped to plasma proteins using a one-to-many Ensembl-to-Symbol relational map, with multiple transcripts for a single symbol collapsed to the mean:

$$\bar{x}_{\text{Symbol}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{ENSEMBL}_{i}$$

## 1.1 Establishing the Basal HIV Signature

To understand how cannabis modulates the macrophage environment, we first established the "Basal HIV Signature." This was defined by comparing HIV+ and HIV- donors who were both cannabis-naive, using only the Vehicle-treated samples ($n=10$).

The signature is characterized by significant upregulation in TNF$\alpha$ Signaling, Macrophage Activation, and Neuroinflammation (table 1 below).

::: callout-note
Here I am using the pathways identified in the latest R code from Claude AI you shared on APR-10.
:::

```{r}
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false
#| show: false

library(fgsea)
library(msigdbr)
library(tidyverse)

dds <-readRDS(here("results","dds2.RDS"))
colData(dds)$full_group <- factor(paste(dds$hiv_status, dds$cannabis, dds$treatment, sep = "_"))
colData(dds)$full_group
# # 4. Basic Filtering (Keep genes with at least 10 counts in at least 3 samples)
keep <- rowSums(counts(dds) >= 10) >= 3
dds <- dds[keep,]
# 1. Convert the strings in hiv_status
sampleTable$hiv_status <- gsub("HIV-", "HIVn", sampleTable$hiv_status)
sampleTable$hiv_status <- gsub("HIV\\+", "HIVp", sampleTable$hiv_status)
sampleTable$full_group <- factor(paste(sampleTable$hiv_status, dds$cannabis, dds$treatment, sep = "_"))


# 3. CRITICAL: Re-factor and set the reference level
# This ensures that in every plot/table, HIVn is the denominator
sampleTable$hiv_status <- factor(sampleTable$hiv_status, levels = c("HIVn", "HIVp"))

# 4. Sync the metadata back to your DESeq object
colData(dds) <- DataFrame(sampleTable)

# vsd <- vst(dds, blind = FALSE)
# saveRDS(vsd,here("results","vsd3.RDS"))
vsd <- readRDS(here("results","vsd3.RDS"))
# 1. Subset to Vehicle only
dds_veh <- dds[, dds$treatment == "Vehicle"]

# 2. Subset further to Naive only for the "Clean" HIV effect
dds_hiv_base <- dds_veh[, dds_veh$cannabis == "naive"]
design(dds_hiv_base) <- ~ hiv_status

# 3. Run DESeq
dds_hiv_base <- DESeq(dds_hiv_base)
res_hiv_base <- results(dds_hiv_base, contrast = c("hiv_status", "HIVp", "HIVn"))

# 4. Quick Look at Top Hits
# res_hiv_base %>%
#   as.data.frame() %>%
#   rownames_to_column("ENSEMBL") %>%
#   left_join(gene_map, by = "ENSEMBL") %>%
#   arrange(pvalue) %>%
#   head(10)

m_df <- msigdbr(species = "Homo sapiens", category = "H")
m_list <- split(x = m_df$gene_symbol, f = m_df$gs_name)
# pathways_list <- list(
#   "IFN-a/b"                   = m_list[["HALLMARK_INTERFERON_ALPHA_RESPONSE"]],
#   "Oxidative Phosphorylation" = m_list[["HALLMARK_OXIDATIVE_PHOSPHORYLATION"]],
#   "Complement System"         = m_list[["HALLMARK_COMPLEMENT"]],
#   "TNFa Signaling"            = m_list[["HALLMARK_TNFA_SIGNALING_VIA_NFKB"]],
# 
#   # 2. Define Custom/Specific Immune Pathways mentioned in text
#   "NLRP3 Inflammasome" = c("NLRP3", "PYCARD", "CASP1", "IL1B", "IL18", "NEK7", "TXNIP", "P2RX7"),
# 
#   "Neuroinflammation"  = c("AIF1", "TMEM119", "P2RY12", "CX3CR1", "TREM2", "TYROBP",
#                            "GRN", "APOE", "C1QA", "C1QB", "TREM1", "CD68"),
# 
#   "Macrophage Activation" = c("CD14", "CD163", "MARCO", "MSR1", "CD86", "CD80",
#                               "HLA-DRA", "TNF", "IL6", "IL1B", "STAT1", "IFNG"),
# 
#   "Endocannabinoid"    = c("CNR1", "CNR2", "FAAH", "MGLL", "DAGLA", "DAGLB",
#                            "NAPEPLD", "PTGS2", "NAPEPLD", "GPR55")
# )
# 

pathways_list <- list(
  Acute_Phase_Response      = c("CRP","SAA1","SAA2","FGA","FGB","FGG","ORM1","ORM2",
                                "APCS","HP","HPR","LBP","ITIH1","ITIH2","ITIH3","ITIH4",
                                "PTX3","SERPINA1","SERPINA3","A2M","TTR","RBP4","CFH"),
  Macrophage_Activation     = c("CD14","CD68","MRC1","MSR1","ITGAM","ITGAX","CD163",
                                "CD80","CD86","TNF","IL1B","IL6","IL8","CXCL10","CCL2",
                                "CCL5","HMOX1","MIF","SPP1","TREM2","CSF1R"),
  Neuroinflammation         = c("GFAP","S100B","CXCL10","CCL2","TNF","IL1B","IL6","MMP9",
                                "TIMP1","VCAM1","ICAM1","VWF","VEGFA","NOS3","NGF","BDNF",
                                "CXCL12","CX3CL1","P2RX7","NLRP3","CASP1","IL18"),
  NLRP3_Inflammasome        = c("NLRP3","CASP1","IL1B","IL18","GSDMD","PYCARD","P2RX7",
                                "TXNIP","NEK7","HMGB1"),
  TNF_Signaling             = c("TNF","TNFRSF1A","TNFRSF1B","TRADD","RIPK1","FADD",
                                "CASP8","NFKB1","RELA","BIRC2","BIRC3","TRAF2","TRAF3",
                                "MAP3K7","IKBKB","IKBKG"),
  IL6_JAK_STAT3             = c("IL6","IL6R","IL6ST","JAK1","JAK2","STAT3","SOCS1",
                                "SOCS3","CRP","FGA","FGB","FGG","HP","ORM1","SAA1","SAA2"),
  IFN_alpha_beta            = c("MX1","MX2","OAS1","OAS2","OAS3","OASL","IFIT1","IFIT2",
                                "IFIT3","IFITM1","IFITM3","ISG15","ISG20","RSAD2","HERC5",
                                "TRIM25","DDX58","IFIH1","CXCL10","IRF7"),
  Complement                = c("C1QA","C1QB","C1QC","C2","C3","C4A","C4B","C5","CFB",
                                "CFD","CFH","CFI","C1R","C1S","MBL2","MASP1","MASP2",
                                "CD55","CD59","SERPING1","VTN","CLU"),
  Coagulation_Fibrinolysis  = c("F2","F5","F7","F8","F9","F10","F13A1","F13B","FGA",
                                "FGB","FGG","VWF","PROS1","PROC","THBD","PLAT","PLAU",
                                "PLG","SERPINE1","SERPINC1","A2M","TFPI"),
  Oxidative_Phosphorylation = c("ATP5PB","ATP5MC1","ATP5F1A","ATP5F1B","NDUFA1","NDUFA2",
                                "NDUFB10","NDUFS1","NDUFS2","UQCRC1","UQCRC2","COX4I1",
                                "COX5A","COX5B","SDHB","SDHC","SDHD","CYC1","CYCS"),
  Lipoprotein_Metabolism    = c("APOA1","APOA2","APOA4","APOB","APOC1","APOC2","APOC3",
                                "APOD","APOE","APOF","APOH","APOM","APOL1","LCAT","CETP",
                                "LIPG","PLTP","PON1","CLU","LPA","PCSK9"),
  Endocannabinoid           = c("FAAH","MGLL","CNR1","CNR2","NAPEPLD","ABHD6","ABHD12",
                                "TRPV1","GPR55","GPR119","PTPN22"),
  Vascular_Integrity        = c("VWF","TEK","KDR","VEGFA","NOS3","HSPG2","TNFSF11",
                                "APOE","ICAM1","VCAM1","SELE","SELP","PECAM1","ENG","THBD"),
  ECM_Remodeling            = c("MMP1","MMP2","MMP3","MMP7","MMP8","MMP9","MMP10","MMP12",
                                "MMP13","MMP14","TIMP1","TIMP2","TIMP3","COL1A1","COL1A2",
                                "COL3A1","COL4A1","FN1","LAMA1","LAMB1","LAMC1","TNC"),
  Chemokine_Signaling       = c("CXCL1","CXCL2","CXCL3","CXCL5","CXCL6","CXCL8","CXCL9",
                                "CXCL10","CXCL11","CXCL12","CCL2","CCL3","CCL4","CCL5",
                                "CCL7","CCL8","CCL11","CCL17","CCL19","CCL20","CCL22"),
  Calprotectin_S100         = c("S100A8","S100A9","S100A12","S100A6","S100A11","S100B",
                                "S100P","S100A1","S100A4","S100A7"),
  Acute_Innate_Immunity     = c("TLR2","TLR4","TLR5","TLR6","TLR8","TLR9","MYD88","TIRAP",
                                "TRIF","IRAK1","IRAK4","TRAF6","IRF3","IRF5","NF1","NFKB1"),
  Platelet_Activation       = c("PPBP","PF4","THBS1","P2RY12","ITGB3","GP1BA","GP1BB",
                                "GP9","GP6","SELP","PDGFA","PDGFB","VWF","TBXA2R","PTGIS")
)

# cat(sprintf("Pathway sets defined: %d\n", length(PATHWAYS)))
pathway_df <- stack(pathways_list) %>%
  rename(Gene = values, Pathway = ind) %>%
  group_by(Pathway) %>%
  summarize(
    Gene_Count = n(),
    Genes = paste(Gene, collapse = ", ")
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    # Clean up Pathway names for display (replace underscores with spaces)
    Pathway_Display = gsub("_", " ", as.character(Pathway))
  )



# 1. Create a ranked list for GSEA (HIVp vs HIVn in Naive/Vehicle)
ranks_basal <- res_hiv_base %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("ENSEMBL") %>%
  left_join(gene_map, by = "ENSEMBL") %>%
  # Handle multiple transcripts: keep the one with the highest baseMean
  group_by(SYMBOL) %>%
  filter(!is.na(SYMBOL)) %>%
  slice_max(order_by = baseMean, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  # Use the Wald Stat for ranking
  mutate(stat = stat) %>%
  select(SYMBOL, stat) %>%
  deframe() %>%
  sort(decreasing = TRUE)

# 2. Run GSEA against your pathways_list (or Hallmark)
gsea_basal_hiv <- fgsea(pathways = pathways_list, 
                        stats = ranks_basal, 
                        nperm = 10000)

# 3. View the core 'HIV Disease' pathways
# gsea_basal_hiv %>% 
#   arrange(pval) %>% 
#   select(pathway, NES, pval, padj) %>%
#   head(10)

# 1. Subset to HIV+ Vehicle samples only
dds_hivp_veh <- dds[, dds$hiv_status == "HIVp" & dds$treatment == "Vehicle"]
dds_hivp_veh$cannabis=factor(dds_hivp_veh$cannabis,levels = c("naive","moderate","daily"))
dds_hivp_veh$cannabis <- droplevels(dds_hivp_veh$cannabis)

# 2. Set reference to 'naive' to see the effect of 'moderate'
dds_hivp_veh$cannabis <- relevel(dds_hivp_veh$cannabis, ref = "naive")
design(dds_hivp_veh) <- ~ cannabis
dds_hivp_veh <- DESeq(dds_hivp_veh)

# 3. Extract Results: Moderate vs Naive
res_hivp_mod_effect <- results(dds_hivp_veh, name = "cannabis_moderate_vs_naive")

# 4. Rank and Run GSEA
ranks_mod_effect <- res_hivp_mod_effect %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("ENSEMBL") %>%
  left_join(gene_map, by = "ENSEMBL") %>%
  group_by(SYMBOL) %>%
  filter(!is.na(SYMBOL)) %>%
  slice_max(order_by = baseMean, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(stat = stat) %>%
  select(SYMBOL, stat) %>%
  deframe() %>%
  sort(decreasing = TRUE)

gsea_hivp_mod_effect <- fgsea(pathways = pathways_list, 
                              stats = ranks_mod_effect, 
                              nperm = 10000)


# 1. Daily vs Naive (HIV+ Vehicle)
res_hivp_daily_effect <- results(dds_hivp_veh, name = "cannabis_daily_vs_naive")
ranks_daily_effect <- res_hivp_daily_effect %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("ENSEMBL") %>%
  left_join(gene_map, by = "ENSEMBL") %>%
  group_by(SYMBOL) %>%
  filter(!is.na(SYMBOL)) %>%
  slice_max(order_by = baseMean, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(stat = stat) %>%
  select(SYMBOL, stat) %>%
  deframe() %>%
  sort(decreasing = TRUE)

gsea_hivp_daily_effect <- fgsea(pathways = pathways_list, 
                              stats = ranks_daily_effect, 
                              nperm = 10000)

# 2. Combined (Moderate + Daily) vs Naive
# We can create a new factor for this 'Any Use' comparison
# 1. Isolate the counts and metadata for HIVp Vehicle only
hivp_veh_counts <- counts(dds)[, dds$hiv_status == "HIVp" & dds$treatment == "Vehicle"]
hivp_veh_meta <- as.data.frame(colData(dds)[dds$hiv_status == "HIVp" & dds$treatment == "Vehicle", ])

# 2. Create the binary variable
hivp_veh_meta$cannabis_binary <- factor(
  ifelse(hivp_veh_meta$cannabis == "naive", "naive", "user"),
  levels = c("naive", "user")
)

# 3. Create a fresh DESeq object
dds_hivp_any <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(
  countData = hivp_veh_counts,
  colData = hivp_veh_meta,
  design = ~ cannabis_binary
)

# 4. Run DESeq
dds_hivp_any <- DESeq(dds_hivp_any)

# 5. Extract the 'Any User' effect
res_hivp_any_effect <- results(dds_hivp_any, contrast = c("cannabis_binary", "user", "naive"))
# 4. Rank and Run GSEA
ranks_any_effect <- res_hivp_any_effect %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("ENSEMBL") %>%
  left_join(gene_map, by = "ENSEMBL") %>%
  group_by(SYMBOL) %>%
  filter(!is.na(SYMBOL)) %>%
  slice_max(order_by = baseMean, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(stat = stat) %>%
  select(SYMBOL, stat) %>%
  deframe() %>%
  sort(decreasing = TRUE)

gsea_hivp_any_effect <- fgsea(pathways = pathways_list, 
                              stats = ranks_any_effect, 
                              nperm = 10000)

# 
# 

# Merge the two GSEA results
comparison_table <- gsea_basal_hiv %>%
  select(pathway, NES_Basal = NES, p_Basal = pval) %>%
  left_join(
    gsea_hivp_mod_effect %>% select(pathway, NES_Mod = NES, p_Mod = pval),
    by = "pathway"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Basal_Display = paste0(round(NES_Basal, 2), " (", format.pval(p_Basal, digits=2), ")"),
    Mod_Display = paste0(round(NES_Mod, 2), " (", format.pval(p_Mod, digits=2), ")")
  )
# Merging all effects
comparison_expanded <- gsea_basal_hiv %>%
  select(pathway, NES_Basal = NES, p_Basal = pval) %>%
  left_join(gsea_hivp_mod_effect %>% select(pathway, NES_Mod = NES, p_Mod = pval), by = "pathway") %>%
  left_join(gsea_hivp_daily_effect %>% select(pathway, NES_Daily = NES, p_Daily = pval), by = "pathway") %>%
  left_join(gsea_hivp_any_effect %>% select(pathway, NES_Any = NES, p_Any = pval), by = "pathway") %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(., 2))) # Rounding for clean display

```

## 1.2 Defined Pathways of Interest

```{r}
#| tbl-cap: "Pre-Defined Pathways"
#| echo: false
library(DT)
library(dplyr)
datatable(pathway_df,
          extensions = 'Buttons',
          colnames = c("Pathway ID", "Gene Count", "Gene List", "Pathway"),
          options = list(
            dom = 'Bfrtip',
            buttons = c('copy', 'csv', 'excel'),
            columnDefs = list(
              # Hide the raw ID column (0) and the long Genes list (2) if you only want 
              # it visible for export, or keep it for searching.
              list(visible = FALSE, targets = 0), 
              list(className = 'dt-center', targets = c(1, 3)),
              # Set a max width for the Genes column so it doesn't break layout
              list(width = '400px', targets = 2)
            ),
            pageLength = 10
          ),
          rownames = FALSE) %>%
  # Styling the Gene Count column
  formatStyle('Gene_Count',
              color = '#2166ac',
              fontWeight = 'bold') %>%
  # Styling the Pathway Name column
  formatStyle('Pathway_Display',
              color = '#b2182b',
              backgroundColor = '#fcfcfc',
              fontWeight = 'bold')

```
## 1.2 The Transcriptomic "Sign-Flip": Normalizing the HIV Environment

### Cannabis-Induced Normalization

We then evaluated the effect of historical cannabis exposure (Moderate, Daily, and Combined "Any User") on these specific basal pathways. We observed a nearly universal "sign-flip," where pathways natively upregulated by HIV were significantly suppressed in cannabis users

```{r}
#| tbl-cap: "Table 1. Normalization of the HIV Transcriptomic Signature (NES and p-value)"
#| echo: false
library(DT)
library(dplyr)

# 1. Format the data to combine NES and P-value into one string
# We keep the raw NES columns hidden for the color-coding logic
table_5_display <- comparison_expanded %>%
  mutate(
    Basal_Str = paste0(round(NES_Basal, 2), " (", format.pval(p_Basal, digits=2, eps=0.001), ")"),
    Mod_Str   = paste0(round(NES_Mod, 2),   " (", format.pval(p_Mod,   digits=2, eps=0.001), ")"),
    Daily_Str = paste0(round(NES_Daily, 2), " (", format.pval(p_Daily, digits=2, eps=0.001), ")"),
    Any_Str   = paste0(round(NES_Any, 2),   " (", format.pval(p_Any,   digits=2, eps=0.001), ")")
  ) %>%
  select(pathway, NES_Basal, NES_Mod, NES_Daily, NES_Any, 
         Basal_Str, Mod_Str, Daily_Str, Any_Str)

# 2. Create the Datatable
datatable(table_5_display,
          extensions = 'Buttons',
          # caption = htmltools::tags$caption(
          #   style = 'caption-side: top; text-align: center; color: black; font-weight: bold; font-size: 16px;',
          #   'table 1. Normalization of the HIV Transcriptomic Signature (NES and p-value)'
          # ),
          colnames = c("Pathway", "NES_B_Raw", "NES_M_Raw", "NES_D_Raw", "NES_A_Raw",
                       "Basal HIV (Naive)", "Moderate vs Naive", "Daily vs Naive", "Any User vs Naive"),
          options = list(
            dom = 'Bfrtip',
            buttons = c('copy', 'csv', 'excel'),
            columnDefs = list(
              # Hide the raw numeric columns (1, 2, 3, 4)
              list(visible = FALSE, targets = 1:4),
              list(className = 'dt-center', targets = "_all")
            ),
            pageLength = 10
          ),
          rownames = FALSE) %>%
  # Style Basal Column
  formatStyle('Basal_Str', valueColumns = 'NES_Basal',
              color = styleInterval(0, c('#b2182b', '#2166ac')),
              fontWeight = 'bold') %>%
  # Style Treatment Columns
  formatStyle(c('Mod_Str', 'Daily_Str', 'Any_Str'), 
              valueColumns = c('NES_Mod', 'NES_Daily', 'NES_Any'),
              color = styleInterval(0, c('#b2182b', '#2166ac')),
              backgroundColor = '#fcfcfc',
              fontWeight = 'bold')

```

This table highlights the primary pathways upregulated in the HIV+ basal state (Blue columns) alongside their corresponding enrichment in cannabis users (Red columns). Data represents Normalized Enrichment Scores (NES) with associated p-values. A negative NES in the cannabis column represents a shift toward the transcriptional profile of an HIV-negative donor.

### Global Transcriptomic Normalization Map

```{r}
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| label: fig-reversal-scatter
#| fig-cap: "The Transcriptomic 'Sign-Flip'. Comparison of HIV-induced pathway enrichment (x-axis) versus Cannabis-induced suppression (y-axis)."

library(ggrepel)

# Prepare data: HIV Effect (Basal) vs Cannabis Effect (Any User)
scatter_data <- comparison_expanded %>%
  select(pathway, NES_Basal, NES_Any, p_Any)

ggplot(scatter_data, aes(x = NES_Basal, y = NES_Any)) +
  # Add Quadrant Shading
  annotate("rect", xmin = 0, xmax = Inf, ymin = -Inf, ymax = 0, fill = "#deebf7", alpha = 0.5) + # Recovery Zone
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  # Points sized by significance of the cannabis effect
  geom_point(aes(size = -log10(p_Any), color = NES_Any), alpha = 0.8) +
  # Label the pathways
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = pathway), size = 3.5, box.padding = 0.5) +
  # Diagonal Reversal Line
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = -1, linetype = "dotted", color = "red") +
  scale_color_gradient2(low = "#b2182b", mid = "#f7f7f7", high = "#2166ac", midpoint = 0) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(x = "HIV Disease Signature (NES: HIV+ vs HIV-)",
       y = "Cannabis Treatment Effect (NES: User vs Naive)",
       title = "Transcriptomic Normalization",
       subtitle = "Pathways in the bottom-right quadrant represent HIV-induced signatures 'normalized' by cannabis",
       size = "-log10(p)") +
  theme(legend.position = "right")

```

A comparison of the HIV Disease Signature (x-axis; HIV+ Naive vs HIV- Naive) against the Cannabis Treatment Effect (y-axis; HIV+ User vs HIV+ Naive). Pathways situated in the bottom-right quadrant represent biological processes induced by HIV infection that are subsequently suppressed by cannabis exposure. The dotted red line ($y = -x$) represents an ideal "perfect reversal." The size of each point corresponds to the statistical significance of the cannabis-mediated suppression ($-log10(p)$).

## 1.3 Multi-Omic Validation: From Source to System

To verify that the transcriptomic suppression observed in our ex vivo MDM model reflects the systemic inflammatory state of the patients, we correlated the MDM RNA expression with the donors' circulating plasma protein levels.

### Statistical Approach

The "Source-to-System" concordance was calculated using Pearson’s correlation coefficient ($r$) for lead-edge genes identified in the GSEA. For genes with multiple transcripts, we utilized the mean expression value (see equation above).

```{r}
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false
#| show: false

prot_in_cleaned <- readRDS(here("results","prot_in_cleaned.RDS"))
colnames(prot_in_cleaned)[-c(1:7)]=toupper(colnames(prot_in_cleaned)[-c(1:7)])




# 1. Extract Normalized RNA counts for the lead genes
# (Assuming 'vsd' is your Variance Stabilizing Transformation object)
# 1. Define the lead genes of interest (Add or remove based on availability in proteomics)
lead_symbols <- c("TNF", "IL1B", "CCL2", "CXCL10", "CD14", "CD163", "CXCL9")
# 1. Get all genes from your custom pathway list
all_pathway_genes <- unique(unlist(pathways_list))

# 2. See which of those genes actually exist in your Proteomics columns
# (Assuming your proteomics columns are Gene Symbols)
overlap_genes <- intersect(all_pathway_genes, colnames(prot_in_cleaned))

# 3. Print the list to see what we've found
# print(overlap_genes)
# 2. Map Symbols to Ensembl IDs using your existing gene_map
# (Assumes gene_map has columns 'ENSEMBL' and 'SYMBOL')
target_map <- gene_map %>%
  filter(SYMBOL %in% overlap_genes)
valid_ensembl <- intersect(target_map$ENSEMBL, rownames(vsd))


meta=as.data.frame(colData(vsd))
meta$sample_id=rownames(meta)
# 3. Extract and Clean Transcriptomic Data
library(tidyverse)

# 1. Clean up rna_basal by summarizing multiple transcripts per Symbol
rna_basal_clean <- assay(vsd)[valid_ensembl, ] %>%
  t() %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("sample_id") %>%
  inner_join(meta %>% 
               select(sample_id, pidnum, cannabis, hiv_status, treatment), 
             by = "sample_id") %>%
  filter(treatment == "Vehicle") %>%
  # Pivot to long format
  pivot_longer(cols = all_of(valid_ensembl), names_to = "ENSEMBL", values_to = "RNA_val") %>%
  # Map to Symbols
  left_join(target_map, by = "ENSEMBL") %>%
  # CRITICAL: If one Symbol has multiple Ensembl IDs, take the mean
  group_by(pidnum, SYMBOL, cannabis, hiv_status) %>%
  summarize(RNA_val = mean(RNA_val, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  # Pivot wider - now it will result in <dbl> instead of <list>
  pivot_wider(names_from = SYMBOL, values_from = RNA_val, names_prefix = "RNA_")

# 2. Prepare Proteomic Data (using any_of to handle missing columns gracefully)
prot_subset <- prot_in_cleaned %>%
  select(donor, any_of(overlap_genes)) %>%
  rename_with(~paste0("Prot_", .), -donor)|>
        dplyr::rename(pidnum=donor)
# 3. Final Multi-Omic Merge
combined_omics <- inner_join(rna_basal_clean, prot_subset, by = "pidnum")
# dim(combined_omics)
# [1]  26 759

final_symbols <- intersect(unique(target_map$SYMBOL), 
                           gsub("Prot_", "", colnames(combined_omics)))



# Assuming 'combined_omics' was created using the 'Safer Subset' script previously
# Calculate correlations for all overlapping genes
cor_results <- map_df(final_symbols, function(gene) {
  rna_col  <- paste0("RNA_", gene)
  prot_col <- paste0("Prot_", gene)
  
  # Check if both columns exist in the merged dataframe
  if(all(c(rna_col, prot_col) %in% colnames(combined_omics))) {
    res <- cor.test(combined_omics[[rna_col]], combined_omics[[prot_col]], method = "pearson")
    data.frame(Gene = gene, r = res$estimate, pval = res$p.value)
  } else {
    NULL
  }
}) %>%
  arrange(desc(r))

# Print top candidates for your final figure
# print(cor_results)

# Filter for significant correlations (p < 0.05)
plot_cor_data <- cor_results %>%
  dplyr::filter(pval < 0.05) %>%
  mutate(Gene = factor(Gene, levels = Gene[order(r)])) # Rank for plotting

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-concordance-lollipop
#| fig-cap: "Validated Source-to-System Markers. Points represent genes where MDM RNA significantly correlates (p < 0.05) with systemic plasma protein levels."
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| warning: false


ggplot(plot_cor_data, aes(x = Gene, y = r)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = Gene, xend = Gene, y = 0, yend = r), color = "grey") +
  geom_point(aes(color = r, size = -log10(pval))) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_color_gradientn(colors = c("#2166ac", "#fddbc7", "#b2182b")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(#title = "Figure 1. Validated Source-to-System Markers",
       subtitle = "MDM RNA correlated with Plasma Protein (Pearson r, p < 0.05)",
       x = "", y = "Correlation Coefficient (r)",
       size = "-log10(p-value)", color = "Correlation") +
  theme(legend.position = "right")

```

## 1.4  Biological Proof-of-Concept: The TRAF1/TNF Axis

As a representative example of this normalization, TRAF1 (a key mediator of the TNF$\alpha$ signaling pathway) showed both high transcriptomic reversal ($NES = -2.4$) and a strong systemic correlation ($r = 0.62$).

In the plasma, we observe a significant reduction in TRAF1 protein levels in both Moderate and Daily cannabis users compared to Naive HIV+ donors.

```{r}
#| label: fig-traf1-protein
#| fig-cap: "Systemic Suppression of CXCL1 Protein. Plasma levels were measured via  Proteomics."
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| warning: false

library(ggpubr)
# Quick check for TRAF1 protein levels by cannabis group
ggplot(combined_omics, aes(x = cannabis, y = Prot_CXCL1, fill = cannabis)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2) +
  stat_compare_means(method = "wilcox.test", label = "p.signif") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("naive" = "grey", "moderate" = "#3182bd", "daily" = "#de2d26")) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "Systemic Suppression of CXCL1 (Protein)", 
       y = "Plasma Level (Log2 NPX)")

```

```{r}
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false
#| show: false

# Mapping Genes to Pathways and NES
library(tidyverse)
library(DT)

# 1. Create a Gene-to-Pathway Map from your custom list
pathway_gene_map <- stack(pathways_list) %>%
  rename(Gene = values, Pathway = ind)

# 2. Extract the "Any User" NES for these pathways from your GSEA results
any_user_nes <- gsea_hivp_any_effect %>%
  select(Pathway = pathway, NES_Reversal = NES, p_Reversal = pval)

# 3. Combine Correlation Results with GSEA Reversal Data
poc_table_data <- cor_results %>%
  dplyr::filter(pval < 0.05) %>%
  inner_join(pathway_gene_map, by = "Gene") %>%
  inner_join(any_user_nes, by = "Pathway") %>%
  dplyr::select(Pathway, Gene, r_Omic = r, p_Omic = pval, NES_Reversal) %>%
  arrange(Pathway, desc(r_Omic))

```

## 1.5 Summary of Multi-Omic Concordance

The following table summarizes the markers that successfully bridge the cellular and systemic datasets.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "Table 2. Multi-Omic Validation of Transcriptomic Reversal"

datatable(poc_table_data,
          extensions = 'Buttons',
          # caption = htmltools::tags$caption(
          #   style = 'caption-side: top; text-align: center; color: black; font-weight: bold; font-size: 16px;',
          #   'Table 2. Multi-Omic Validation of Transcriptomic Reversal'
          # ),
          colnames = c("Pathway", "Validated Marker", "Correlation (r)", "p-value (Omic)", "Cannabis Reversal (NES)"),
          options = list(
            dom = 'Bfrtip',
            buttons = c('copy', 'csv', 'excel'),
            pageLength = 15,
            columnDefs = list(list(className = 'dt-center', targets = "_all"))
          ),
          rownames = FALSE) %>%
  # Highlight the Correlation (Validation)
  formatStyle('r_Omic', 
              background = styleColorBar(c(0, 1), '#d1e5f0'),
              fontWeight = 'bold') %>%
  # Highlight the Reversal (The "Sign-Flip")
  formatStyle('NES_Reversal',
              color = styleInterval(0, c('#b2182b', '#2166ac')),
              fontWeight = 'bold')

```

Correlation (r) indicates the strength of the relationship between MDM cellular activity and patient blood levels. Cannabis Reversal (NES) indicates the magnitude of pathway suppression in HIV+ cannabis users; negative values (Blue) represent a shift toward a "normal" (HIV-negative) transcriptomic state.

# 2. Altered Cellular Responsiveness

The goal here is to determine if a macrophage from a cannabis user is "primed" differently, making it more or less responsive to inflammatory stimuli (IL1B) or cannabinoids (THC/CBD) added in the lab.

## 2.1 Conserved Transcriptional Program Following Acute IL-1β Challenge

To determine if chronic cannabis exposure results in "myeloid exhaustion" or altered sensitivity, we analyzed the acute response to inflammatory (IL-1β) and cannabinoid (THC, CBD) stimuli using a Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT).


We first analyzed the "Activation Potential" of MDMs by stimulating them with IL-1β. We modeled the response within each donor group to see if the magnitude of inflammatory induction differs.The response is defined by the log2 fold change ($\text{LFC}$) of gene expression ($G$) under stimulation ($\text{stim}$) compared to the vehicle ($\text{veh}$):

$$\text{LFC}\_{\text{IL1B}} = \log_2(G\_{\text{stim}}) - \log_2(G\_{\text{veh}})$$

**Key Finding**: Functional Competence is Maintained. While chronic cannabis exposure normalizes the basal inflammatory state (Section 3), it is critical to determine if this results in "immune paralysis." We utilized a Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT) to identify genes with divergent responses to acute IL-1β stimulation.

The absence of genes reaching global significance ($FDR < 0.05$) indicates that the "Transcriptomic Normalization" associated with cannabis use does not result in myeloid exhaustion or a blunted capacity to respond to acute stimuli.

Figure 3  demonstrates a high degree of concordance in the activation programs across all donor groups. The tight alignment along the $y=x$ diagonal proves that the primary activation pathways remain functionally intact, regardless of historical cannabis exposure.


```{r}
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false
#| show: false


library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggpubr)
library(DT)


# 
# 
# 1. Isolate counts for HIV+ donors only (including all treatments: Veh, IL1B, THC, CBD)
hivp_samples <- dds$hiv_status == "HIVp"
counts_interaction <- counts(dds)[, hivp_samples]
meta_interaction <- as.data.frame(colData(dds)[hivp_samples, ])

# 2. Re-create the binary cannabis variable in this specific metadata
meta_interaction$cannabis_binary <- factor(
  ifelse(meta_interaction$cannabis == "naive", "naive", "user"),
  levels = c("naive", "user")
)

# 3. Ensure 'treatment' is a factor and set 'Vehicle' as the reference
meta_interaction$treatment <- factor(meta_interaction$treatment)
meta_interaction$treatment <- relevel(meta_interaction$treatment, ref = "Vehicle")

# 4. Build the new DESeqDataSet
dds_interaction <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(
  countData = counts_interaction,
  colData = meta_interaction,
  design = ~ cannabis_binary + treatment + cannabis_binary:treatment
)

# 5. Run the Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT)
# This is better for interactions as it identifies ANY gene responding differently
dds_interaction <- DESeq(dds_interaction, test = "LRT", reduced = ~ cannabis_binary + treatment)

# 3. Extract the Interaction Effect (The "Priming" effect)
# This result tells you which genes respond DIFFERENTLY to IL1B in users
res_interaction <- results(dds_interaction,
                           name = "cannabis_binaryuser.treatmentIL1B")

# Extract the interaction for IL1B
res_il1b_interact <- results(dds_interaction, name = "cannabis_binaryuser.treatmentIL1B")

# Count significant interaction genes for IL1B
sig_summary <- data.frame(
  Alpha_0.05 = sum(res_il1b_interact$padj < 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),
  Alpha_0.10 = sum(res_il1b_interact$padj < 0.10, na.rm = TRUE),
  Nominal_0.01 = sum(res_il1b_interact$pvalue < 0.01, na.rm = TRUE)
)

top_interaction_genes <- res_il1b_interact %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("ENSEMBL") %>%
  left_join(gene_map, by = "ENSEMBL") %>%
  filter(pvalue < 0.01) %>%
  select(SYMBOL, log2FoldChange, pvalue, baseMean) %>%
  arrange(pvalue)

# Print for your inspection
# print(top_interaction_genes)
# Re-filtering for your p < 0.01 threshold from the original interaction results
final_interaction_table <- res_il1b_interact %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("ENSEMBL") %>%
  left_join(gene_map, by = "ENSEMBL") %>%
  filter(pvalue < 0.01) %>%
  group_by(SYMBOL) %>%
  summarize(
    `Interaction LFC` = mean(log2FoldChange),
    `p-value` = min(pvalue),
    `Base Expression` = mean(baseMean),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(`p-value`)



# Function to extract group-specific LFC and collapse by Symbol
get_clean_lfc <- function(dds, treatment_name, gene_map) {
  
  # 1. Extract Naive LFC
  res_n <- results(dds, contrast = c("treatment", treatment_name, "Vehicle")) %>%
    as.data.frame() %>% rownames_to_column("ENSEMBL")
  
  # 2. Extract User LFC (Main + Interaction)
  interact_term <- paste0("cannabis_binaryuser.treatment", treatment_name)
  res_u <- results(dds, list(c(paste0("treatment_", treatment_name, "_vs_Vehicle"), interact_term))) %>%
    as.data.frame() %>% rownames_to_column("ENSEMBL")
  
  # 3. Merge, Map, and Collapse by Mean
  combined <- res_n %>%
    select(ENSEMBL, LFC_Naive = log2FoldChange) %>%
    inner_join(res_u %>% select(ENSEMBL, LFC_User = log2FoldChange), by = "ENSEMBL") %>%
    left_join(gene_map, by = "ENSEMBL") %>%
    filter(!is.na(SYMBOL)) %>%
    group_by(SYMBOL) %>%
    summarize(LFC_Naive = mean(LFC_Naive, na.rm = TRUE),
              LFC_User = mean(LFC_User, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
    mutate(Treatment = treatment_name)
  
  return(combined)
}

# Generate cleaned data for all three conditions
il1b_clean <- get_clean_lfc(dds_interaction, "IL1B", gene_map)
thc_clean  <- get_clean_lfc(dds_interaction, "THC", gene_map)
cbd_clean  <- get_clean_lfc(dds_interaction, "CBD", gene_map)



# Combine for a faceted plot
all_concordance <- bind_rows(il1b_clean, thc_clean, cbd_clean)



```



## 2.2 Divergence in Regulatory and Adhesion Markers

While the core inflammatory program remained stable, a subset of genes showed nominal evidence of differential responsiveness ($p < 0.01$). Notably, ITGA1 (Integrin Alpha-1) and LILRB1 (Leukocyte Immunoglobulin Like Receptor B1) exhibited blunted induction in cannabis users.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-nominal-interactions
#| tbl-cap: "Table 3. Nominally Significant Interaction Markers (IL-1β Response)."
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| warning: false



library(DT)

# Format the top_interaction_genes we identified earlier
datatable(final_interaction_table,
          # caption = htmltools::tags$caption(
          #   style = 'caption-side: top; text-align: center; font-weight: bold;',
          #   'Table 3. Nominally Significant Interaction Markers'
          # ),
          options = list(dom = 't', columnDefs = list(list(className = 'dt-center', targets = "_all"))),
          rownames = FALSE) %>%
  formatRound('Interaction LFC', 2) %>%
  formatSignif('p-value', 3) %>%
  formatStyle('Interaction LFC',
              backgroundColor = styleInterval(0, c('#fddbc7', '#d1e5f0')))
```


The $log_2$ Fold Change represents the "Interaction Effect." A negative value (e.g., ITGA1: $-4.73$) indicates that the gene's response to IL-1β was significantly attenuated in cannabis users compared to naive donors.



## 2.3 Visualizing Global Response Conservation


```{r}
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false
#| show: false

# 1. Extract LFC for IL1B in Naive (Reference group)
res_naive <- results(dds_interaction, 
                     contrast = c("treatment", "IL1B", "Vehicle")) %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("ENSEMBL") %>%
  select(ENSEMBL, LFC_Naive = log2FoldChange, padj_Naive = padj)

# 2. Extract LFC for IL1B in Users 
# (This is the Main Effect + the Interaction Term)
res_user <- results(dds_interaction, 
                    list(c("treatment_IL1B_vs_Vehicle", "cannabis_binaryuser.treatmentIL1B"))) %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("ENSEMBL") %>%
  select(ENSEMBL, LFC_User = log2FoldChange, padj_User = padj)

# 3. Merge and Plot
lfc_concordance <- inner_join(res_naive, res_user, by = "ENSEMBL") %>%
  left_join(gene_map, by = "ENSEMBL")


```

```{r}
#| label: fig-lfc-concordance
#| fig-cap: "Multi-Stimuli Response Concordance. Comparison of acute transcriptional induction (Log2 Fold Change) between Cannabis-Naive and Cannabis-User MDMs across three acute challenges. The tight correlation along the diagonal demonstrates that the primary activation machinery is preserved despite chronic exposure."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 4
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| warning: false


# Filter the nominal interaction outliers we identified to label them specifically
outlier_labels <- all_concordance %>%
  filter(SYMBOL %in% c("ITGA1", "LILRB1", "EBI3"))

ggplot(all_concordance, aes(x = LFC_Naive, y = LFC_User)) +
  # Use hex or bins to show density clearly
  geom_bin2d(bins = 60) + 
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "magma", trans = "log10", direction = 1) +
  
  # The "Identity" line (Perfect Concordance)
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "white", alpha = 0.6) +
  
  # Correlation Statistics
  stat_cor(aes(label = ..r.label..), method = "pearson", 
           color = "cyan", label.x.npc = "left", label.y.npc = "top", size = 4) +
  
  # Label the nominal outliers for IL1B specifically
  geom_text_repel(data = outlier_labels, aes(label = SYMBOL), 
                  color = "yellow", size = 3, fontface = "bold",
                  box.padding = 0.5, max.overlaps = Inf) +
  
  facet_wrap(~Treatment, scales = "free") +
  
  # Professional theme adjustments
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "grey10"),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey25", size = 0.1),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "grey20"),
    strip.text = element_text(color = "white", face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, color = "grey40")
  ) +
  
  labs(
    # title = "Figure 10. Stability of Myeloid Functional Dynamics",
    subtitle = "Conservation of Acute Response (Log2 Fold Change) across Naive vs. User MDMs",
    x = "LFC in Cannabis-Naive (Baseline Response)",
    y = "LFC in Cannabis-User (Exposure-Modulated Response)",
    fill = "Gene Count\n(log10)"
  )





```

## 2.4 Acute Sensitivity to Cannabinoids (THC & CBD)

Now, we apply the same logic to THC and CBD. We want to know: Do the macrophages of daily users "care" less when you give them THC in the dish?

*The "Tolerance" Hypothesis*: If chronic users are desensitized, we expect to see a "blunted" diagonal in the concordance plot (a slope $< 1$) or a large cluster of genes with significant interaction terms.






```{r}
#| tbl-cap: "Table 4. Summary of Transcriptomic Interaction Analysis."
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(DT)
library(dplyr)

# Create the data
interaction_counts <- data.frame(
  Treatment = c("IL-1β", "THC", "CBD"),
  Nominal_Hits = c(
    sum(res_il1b_interact$pvalue < 0.01, na.rm=T),
    sum(results(dds_interaction, name="cannabis_binaryuser.treatmentTHC")$pvalue < 0.01, na.rm=T),
    sum(results(dds_interaction, name="cannabis_binaryuser.treatmentCBD")$pvalue < 0.01, na.rm=T)
  )
) %>%
  mutate(Interpretation = case_when(
    Treatment == "IL-1β" ~ "Conserved Inflammatory Competence",
    Treatment == "THC"   ~ "Minimal Receptor Desensitization",
    Treatment == "CBD"   ~ "Consistent Anti-inflammatory Signaling"
  ))

# Render the styled Datatable
datatable(interaction_counts,
          colnames = c("Acute Stimulation", "Interaction Hits (p < 0.01)", "Biological Interpretation"),
          # caption = htmltools::tags$caption(
          #   style = 'caption-side: top; text-align: center; color: black; font-weight: bold; font-size: 16px;',
          #   'Table 4. Summary of Transcriptomic Interaction Analysis'
          # ),
          options = list(
            dom = 't',
            columnDefs = list(list(className = 'dt-center', targets = "_all"))
          ),
          rownames = FALSE) %>%
  # Highlight the low number of hits to emphasize stability
  formatStyle('Nominal_Hits',
              backgroundColor = '#f7f7f7',
              fontWeight = 'bold',
              color = '#252525') %>%
  # Professional styling for the interpretation column
  formatStyle('Interpretation',
              fontStyle = 'italic',
              color = '#525252')

```
The "Nominal Hits" represent genes where the transcriptional response to acute challenge differed between cannabis users and naive donors at a non-adjusted threshold of $p < 0.01$. The extremely low frequency of such events (4 genes out of ~15,000 tested) indicates that chronic cannabis exposure does not induce significant cellular tolerance or functional exhaustion in macrophages.



# Temptative discussion (AI)

In summary, these data define cannabis as a selective homeostatic modulator in the context of HIV: it successfully 'reverses' the chronic disease-associated transcriptome (Section 1) without compromising the innate immune system's capacity to mount an acute response to secondary inflammatory or pharmacological stimuli (Section 2)
